stories.headline: Update: Highlands Ranch driver formally charged with vehicular homicide after sidewalk crash kills one, injures two
stories.summary:
Adam Robert Bauserman, 28, was formally charged Thursday by the 23rd Judicial District Attorney's Office in connection with a Monday crash on Wildcat Reserve Parkway in Highlands Ranch that killed Corrine McIntosh More, 35, and seriously injured two other pedestrians. Bauserman appeared via video conference Friday in Douglas County Court, dressed in an orange jumpsuit, to hear the charges against him.

The criminal complaint includes one count of vehicular homicide, two counts of vehicular assault, one count of second-degree assault, one count of reckless driving, and three counts of leaving the scene of a crash. Witnesses told police they believed Bauserman may have intentionally driven his truck onto the sidewalk at a high rate of speed before fleeing the scene; he was arrested approximately 15 minutes later a few miles away.

Bauserman had a suspended license at the time of the crash stemming from an April 2025 DUI in which his blood alcohol content was more than twice the legal limit. He remains held at the Douglas County jail on $100,000 bond and is due back in court next Friday.

stories.headline: Polis vetoes bill that would have allowed Coloradans to sue ICE agents for civil rights violations
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis vetoed Senate Bill 26-005, the Rights Violation in Immigration Enforcement Remedy bill, which would have allowed Colorado citizens to sue federal immigration enforcement officers for civil rights violations within two years of an alleged incident. Polis cited constitutional concerns about the legislation's potential conflict with federal supremacy law and its narrow scope limited to civil immigration enforcement.

"I applaud the sponsors of this legislation for tackling this critical issue," Polis wrote in his veto message. "Unfortunately, after careful consideration, I believe the legal risks of the actual language in SB 26-005 outweigh the potential benefits." The governor noted he would have been more likely to support a broader bill covering federal officer misconduct across multiple contexts, including protests, elections, prisons, and workplaces.

A broader companion measure, SB 26-176, died in the legislature after several Democratic lawmakers joined Republicans to defeat it, which Polis attributed to "overly intense and misleading lobbying from local governments and public entities." The veto is part of a pattern of Polis breaking with his own party during the final legislative session of his governorship.

stories.headline: Polis signs law creating enterprise to lower homeowners insurance costs through hail-resistant roof incentives
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis signed SB26-155, the Increase Access Homeowner's Insurance Enterprise bill, which creates a new state enterprise aimed at making homeowners insurance more affordable in Colorado. Polis has said the measure is part of a broader effort he expects will save Coloradans $800 per year on insurance costs.

The new enterprise will be governed by a seven-member board with expertise spanning home hardening, risk mitigation, resilient roof systems, and insurance underwriting. Beginning in 2027, it will be funded by an annual fee of 0.5% of total premiums collected by admitted insurers selling multiperil homeowner's insurance policies in the state.

Revenues will go toward incentivizing hail-resistant roofing and other loss-mitigation efforts, and are intended to increase market competition in high-wildfire-risk areas where insurers have curtailed coverage in recent years. The bill was sponsored by House Speaker Julie McCluskie and Rep. Kyle Brown along with Senators Kyle Mullica and Janice Marchman.

stories.headline: Federal managers to impose 10-year Colorado River management plan with mandatory two-year renegotiations
stories.summary:
Lacking consensus among the seven Colorado River basin states, the U.S. Bureau of Reclamation will impose its own framework for managing the river, featuring mandatory renegotiation every two years to account for shifting water supplies, Acting Commissioner Scott Cameron announced Thursday at a University of Colorado Boulder conference. It marks the first time federal officials have publicly described their final management approach for the river.

"I wish I could tell you that we have a solution," Cameron told attendees, which included several state negotiators. "As you are very painfully aware, we do not have a solution, at least at this point." For more than two years, basin-state negotiators have failed to reach agreement as drought and overuse continue to shrink the river's supply.

The two-year renegotiation cycle is intended to give river managers flexibility to adapt to rapidly changing conditions. All parties have agreed that a consensus seven-state agreement would be the preferable outcome, but federal officials say they cannot wait indefinitely for states to reach that consensus. Colorado depends heavily on the Colorado River for water supply across its Western Slope and Front Range communities.

stories.headline: Aurora photo-radar program may violate state law requiring 45-day response window for speeding tickets
stories.summary:
A Denver-area computer repair specialist, Jason Anderson, discovered through AI-assisted legal research that Aurora's mobile speed camera program gives ticketed drivers only 30 days to respond — potentially violating a 2023 state law requiring a minimum 45-day response period. Aurora has issued 26,268 tickets and collected $627,000 since launching the program in late December, with roughly 60% of violators having paid.

Anderson challenged his $40 ticket and a magistrate commended his research but declined to dismiss it, and the city maintained it is a home-rule municipality with the constitutional authority to set its own timelines. However, two sponsors of the 2023 state law — former Reps. Leslie Herod and Meg Froelich — said the 45-day provision was deliberately included and that Aurora should comply.

"If you want everyone to follow the laws, you need to follow the laws, too," Anderson said, adding that he hopes the city will be required to refund fines paid by motorists who received the shorter-deadline notices. The dispute illustrates ongoing tension between Colorado's home-rule city authority and statewide legislation governing automated traffic enforcement.

stories.headline: CDOT urges motorists to inspect vehicles as dry conditions heighten vehicle-caused wildfire risk
stories.summary:
The Colorado Department of Transportation is urging drivers to inspect their vehicles before hitting the road this summer, warning that hot brakes, dragging chains, and discarded cigarettes are among the leading causes of highway-corridor wildfires. Of the roughly 6,000 wildfires Colorado experiences each year, more than 90% are caused by people, according to Colorado Division of Fire Prevention and Control Executive Director Mike Morgan.

"The drought conditions, the lack of snowpack — these are unprecedented conditions," Morgan said. "It just scares the heck out of me." CDOT launched an emergency tree-trimming project along high-risk state highways two weeks ago and pointed to a January Sedgwick County grass fire traced to a semi-truck with its landing gear dragging on I-76 as a recent example of the hazard.

Officials recommend drivers regularly inspect tires and chains, avoid stopping in grassy areas where hot catalytic converters could ignite vegetation, and carry a Class A fire extinguisher. The warnings come as Colorado enters summer under drought conditions, with Morgan cautioning residents not to be lulled by recent rainfall: "At this point in time, what it looks like, I would call that false hope."

stories.headline: Denver forecast: Near-record heat Saturday, with highs in the mid-90s expected through the weekend
stories.summary:
Denver is expected to approach record high temperatures Saturday, with metro-area highs projected to reach the mid-90s through the weekend, according to the National Weather Service in Boulder. Friday's high is forecast near 90 degrees before temperatures climb further.

The forecast is mostly dry for the metro area, though isolated storms may develop east of Denver in the late afternoon hours. The heat comes as Colorado faces an ongoing drought and elevated wildfire risk across the state.

Residents are advised to take precautions during the heat, particularly given the combination of high temperatures and low humidity that increases fire danger across the region.

stories.headline: Denver-area small landlords hold steady amid soft rental market, but rising costs squeeze profit margins
stories.summary:
While large Denver apartment operators have resorted to aggressive rent concessions as metro vacancy rates peaked near 7% and year-over-year rents declined between 3.6% and 5%, independent landlords renting single-family homes and small multi-unit properties are experiencing a markedly different market. A new TurboTenant State of the Rental Industry 2026 report, from the Denver-based company, found that about 90% of independent landlords nationwide are not offering move-in incentives.

Single-family rentals are outperforming the broader Denver market, with well-located homes projected to see modest rent growth of 2–3% in 2026, even as apartments remain flat or decline. Three in four landlords surveyed nationally reported higher operating costs than a year ago, with most absorbing those costs rather than passing them to tenants — a choice that maintains occupancy but compresses profit margins.

The outlook for Denver's rental market is cautiously optimistic as the development pipeline slows to its lowest level in a decade. Nearly 60% of landlords nationally plan to hold their properties rather than sell or exit, and annual rent growth is forecast to reach 2–3% as supply moderates and demand recovers.

stories.headline: Parker named Colorado's best place to live by U.S. News, with several other Denver suburbs in top 250 nationally
stories.summary:
Parker has been ranked Colorado's best place to live by U.S. News & World Report, finishing 87th nationally in the publication's annual rankings of hundreds of U.S. cities. The Douglas County town of approximately 66,000 residents scored well for quality of life, job market, and property values, with a median household income of $139,826 and a median home value of $615,198 — well above national medians.

Centennial, Castle Rock, and Arvada also placed in the top 250 nationally, and 18 of the 19 ranked Colorado municipalities were located along the Front Range, with the majority in the Denver metro area. Loveland and Fort Collins ranked 11th and 12th in the state, respectively.

U.S. News & World Report's rankings are based on four factors: quality of life, value, desirability, and job market. The methodology this year favored Heartland and Southern communities nationally, with the top 19 U.S. places concentrated in the Great Lakes, Midwest, and South regions.

stories.headline: Launch Family Entertainment to open first Colorado location in Centennial
stories.summary:
Launch Family Entertainment, a Rhode Island-based franchise, plans to open its first Colorado location in Centennial in 2027, offering bowling, trampolines, and arcade games. The entertainment center will serve the south Denver suburban market, which includes a dense concentration of families in Douglas and Arapahoe counties.

The opening would mark the chain's entry into the competitive Colorado family entertainment market. No specific address or square footage was disclosed in the announcement.

stories.headline: Rockies open home series against Brewers looking to extend three-series winning streak
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies will host the Milwaukee Brewers for a three-game series beginning Friday night at Coors Field, seeking their third consecutive series victory after taking two of three from both the San Francisco Giants and the Los Angeles Angels. Ryan Feltner (2-1, 4.85 ERA) will start for Colorado in just his second outing since returning from the injured list following right ulnar nerve inflammation.

Feltner was sharp in his return, tossing six scoreless innings on just 63 pitches in an 8-3 win over San Francisco last weekend. Milwaukee counters with rookie Brandon Sproat (1-4, 6.24 ERA), who has struggled to complete five innings in five of his last six starts despite the Brewers having won 19 of their last 26 games and holding a 4.5-game lead in the NL Central.

"Win more baseball games. I think we all want to do that. I think we're headed in the right direction," Rockies catcher Hunter Goodman said this week. The Rockies, who lost 119 games last season, have shown improvement but face a formidable opponent in the surging Brewers.

stories.headline: Ringo Starr brings All Starr Band to Denver, reflecting on Beatles, country music revival at 85
stories.summary:
Ringo Starr, 85, is bringing his All Starr Band to Denver as part of a new tour supporting his 22nd studio album, "Long Long Road," a country-flavored record produced by T Bone Burnett. Starr spoke with The Denver Post ahead of the show, recounting the band's origins in 1989 and its evolution into a showcase for hits from an rotating cast of accomplished musicians, including current bandmates Colin Hay, Steve Lukather, and Hamish Stewart.

Starr's recent resurgence includes his 2025 country album "Look Up," which gave him his first No. 1 solo release in the United Kingdom, and a performance at the Grand Ole Opry. He also recorded a duet with Paul McCartney — their first-ever collaboration — on McCartney's new album. Starr's connection to Colorado dates to a 1964 Beatles concert at Red Rocks Amphitheatre.

The All Starr Band's set list is designed for maximum audience singalong, featuring each bandmember's signature hits alongside Starr's own catalog, including Beatles classics such as "With a Little Help from My Friends" and "Octopus's Garden." The tour reflects a late-career creative momentum unusual for an artist of Starr's vintage.

weather.notable: About 12 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: Sometimes the road less traveled is less traveled for a reason.

topStory.headline: Becerra Advances to California Governor General Election as Second Spot Remains Undecided
topStory.summary:
Democrat Xavier Becerra has secured a place in the November general election for California governor, the Associated Press projected Friday, following Tuesday's primary election in the nation's most populous state. With 65% of expected votes counted as of Friday evening, Becerra held 26.7% of the vote, narrowly ahead of Republican Steve Hilton at 26.4% and Democrat Tom Steyer at 21%.

Becerra, a California native, served more than 35 years in public office, including as California's state attorney general and as U.S. Secretary of Health and Human Services under President Joe Biden. He campaigned on his extensive government experience and pledged to oppose President Donald Trump's policies while freezing insurance and utility rates for Californians. On election night, Becerra said, 'I am ready to lead the fight to uphold California's promise to make sure we have the governance worthy of our gifts.'

The race to determine Becerra's November opponent remains unresolved between Hilton and Steyer, with ballot counting expected to continue for weeks. California sends mail-in ballots to all 23 million registered voters, with an estimated 80% of votes cast by mail. Each county has up to 30 days after the election to count ballots, and results will not be certified until July 10.

The delayed count prompted President Trump to allege without evidence on social media that Democrats were attempting to 'steal' the California gubernatorial and Los Angeles mayoral primaries. The First Assistant U.S. Attorney in Los Angeles, Bill Essayli, said Friday that 'multiple election fraud investigations are underway,' though no specifics were provided. California Democratic Party Chairperson Rusty Hicks called Trump's claims 'baseless,' and Becerra responded directly on social media, saying, 'The voters decide who leads California. Not you.'

If elected governor in November, Becerra would become California's first Latino governor since 1875, in a state where Latinos comprise approximately 41% of the population. The race is the most expensive California gubernatorial contest on record, driven by heavy advertising spending from Steyer and Silicon Valley-backed support for San Jose Mayor Matt Mahan. Democrats have governed California continuously since 2011, and current Governor Gavin Newsom is term-limited from seeking reelection.
